justification: The chapter argues early Christians framed Jesus using Greek categories (Opening lines 47-50, Justin Martyr comparing to sons of Zeus). But the specific Dennis McDonald mimesis argument — Mark's anointing scene echoing Homer's Euroclea with the 'fame far and wide' pun — is not in the chapter inventory. The Homeric parallel is specific new evidence for the existing argument.

Chapter 3 'Opening (lines 47-50)' covers Justin Martyr's First Apology 21-22 where Christians 'propound nothing different from what you believe regarding those whom you esteem sons of Zeus' including Heracles, Asclepius, Dionysus, Perseus. The finding that Jesus was positioned competitively against Greek savior deities (Serapis, Orpheus, Dionysus) is the same argument with the same type of evidence — early Christian competitive positioning against Greek deities. The specific deities differ (Serapis/Orpheus vs Heracles/Asclepius) but the argument and evidence category are identical.

Chapter 3 'Opening (lines 47-50)' explicitly covers Justin Martyr's First Apology 21-22 comparing Jesus to Heracles, Asclepius, Dionysus, Perseus. This finding presents the same Justin Martyr apologetic comparing Jesus to Mercury (Logos), Dionysus, Asclepius, and Heracles. Same source, same argument, same evidence.

The chapter argues Jesus's titles and theology are Hellenistic royal-political constructs (throughout). But the specific evidence of Tammuz/Ishtar death-resurrection cycle, spring equinox mourning parallels, Persephone/Osiris/Dionysus patterns is not in the inventory. The QA global decision rejects 'mystery cult' framing but accepts institutional data. The specific Tammuz/Ishtar/Persephone cycle evidence is new evidence for the existing argument that Christianity drew on Greek/Near Eastern frameworks.

The chapter argues Jesus's death-resurrection should be understood in Greco-Roman political terms. The specific claim that crucifixion was exclusively a Roman execution practice (foreign to Judaism, considered an abomination) is not in the chapter inventory but supports the existing argument. New evidence for the existing Greco-Roman framing.

The chapter argues 'Son of God' is Hellenistic royal theology and covers divine sonship across dynasties. The Adonis cycle (death-resurrection, mourning by women, spring equinox celebration, blood as eternal life) is specific Greco-Mediterranean evidence not in the inventory. Per QA global decision, the institutional data is valid but must be reframed through imperial cult, not mystery cult.

The chapter covers Jesus's birth as a new star (lines 620-633) and 'Messiah' as anointed title (lines 445-464). But the specific claim that 'anointed one' appears in Mesopotamian-Hellenistic cuneiform astronomical nomenclature is not in the inventory. This is new evidence connecting the star/anointing motifs through Mesopotamian-Hellenistic astronomy.

Chapter 3 'Opening (lines 47-50)' covers Justin Martyr explicitly naming Dionysus as a son of Zeus parallel to Jesus. The 'God manifested in flesh' section (lines 505-512) covers Epiphanes/Epiphany as Hellenistic divine manifestation title (Antiochus IV, Ptolemy V). The combination of Dionysus as 'son of Zeus' with divine manifestation/Epiphany is covered by these two inventory items together.

The chapter does not discuss Simon Magus/Simonians or their application of trinitarian theology and divine titles. The Simonian movement as a parallel case where Hellenistic divine kingship concepts were applied to a non-Jesus figure is neither argued nor evidenced in any chapter inventory. The evidence (Simonians using trinitarian concepts, identifying Simon with Zeus-like deity Semo Sanchez) is absent.

The chapter does not argue that Jesus was a mystagogue or teacher of mysteries. Per QA global decision, 'mystery cult' framing is rejected in favor of imperial cult under foreign rule. The specific argument that Mark 14's Greek vocabulary identifies Jesus as a mystagogue using Hellenistic mystery religion terms is a new argument. However, it conflicts with the QA decision rejecting mystery cult framing. The institutional data (Greek vocabulary in Mark 14) could be reframed, but the mystagogue argument itself is new.

The chapter argues Gospel writers framed Jesus using Greek literary and institutional frameworks (Opening lines 47-50, Justin Martyr). The specific Odysseus-demoniac parallel (asking names, 'Nobody' vs 'Legion', same story structure) from Dennis McDonald's Homeric mimesis work is not in the inventory. New specific textual evidence for the existing argument about Greek literary framing.

Chapter 3 'Opening (lines 22-57)' explicitly lists 'Augustus as divi filius on coins and civic festivals' as evidence for the argument that titles given to Jesus were pre-existing Greek/Roman political terms. Same argument, same evidence.

The chapter does not discuss Beelzebub/Baal-zebub as a title of Zeus or Jesus's authority over demons in relation to Greek underworld hierarchy. This is a new argument (Jesus's exorcistic authority understood in relation to Greek deities) with new evidence (Baal-zebub as an epithet of Zeus at Pergamon). Not in any chapter inventory.

Chapter 3 'Opening (lines 47-50)' covers Justin Martyr comparing Jesus to multiple sons of Zeus (Heracles, Asclepius, Dionysus, Perseus), explicitly arguing that early Christians positioned Jesus within existing Greek divine-son archetypes. The finding that Jesus is a 'composite' of Apollo/Osiris is the same argument — Jesus modeled on multiple Hellenistic divine figures — with the same category of evidence.

Chapter 3 'Alexander the Great (lines 574-580)' covers Alexander's divine cult, and the entire dynasty sections (lines 574-609) systematically document how Hellenistic rulers were deified. The Augustus example is covered in 'Opening (lines 22-57)'. The finding restates the same argument (apotheosis was standard) with the same type of evidence (Augustus bringing peace and being considered divine). Covered.

The chapter presents apotheosis evidence (Alexander, Ptolemies, Augustus in lines 574-609) but does NOT explicitly argue the distinction between individual Greco-Roman apotheosis and collective Jewish resurrection as a separate theological category. The evidence (Romulus, Alexander apotheosis) already appears, but this specific argument — that apotheosis and resurrection are separate conceptual categories from different traditions — is not made.

Chapter 3 'Opening (lines 22-57)' explicitly lists 'Augustus as divi filius on coins and civic festivals' and 'Evidence from inscriptions and coins (lines 610-619)' covers coins with 'theos sebastos Kaisar'. The finding about Augustus deification coins is the same evidence already in the inventory.

The chapter does not argue that Christian power-over-serpents theology (Luke 10:19, Numbers 21:5-9) parallels Asclepius's rod or Mercury's caduceus. This is a new argument (Greek mythological symbolism shaped Christian power claims) with new evidence (rod of Asclepius, caduceus of Mercury, Luke 10:19 connection). Not in any chapter inventory.

The chapter argues Jesus and Christianity used Greek frameworks. The specific evidence of Eleusinian mysteries (Demeter as grain, Bacchus as grape, consuming the god, death-rebirth tropes) from a rabbi's perspective is not in the inventory. Per QA global decision, the institutional data is valid but must be reframed through imperial cult, not mystery cult. New evidence for existing argument.

Chapter 3 'Alexander the Great (lines 574-580)' explicitly covers Alexander's oracle at Siwa/Zeus-Ammon and divine sonship claim. 'Ptolemaic Dynasty (lines 581-587)' covers Ptolemaic divine kingship including Serapis cult. The finding restates these exact covered points.

Chapter 3 'Ptolemaic Dynasty (lines 581-587)' mentions Ptolemy I initiating the cult of Serapis (Tacitus, Histories 4.83-84), but does NOT detail Serapis as a composite of Zeus, Pluto, Asclepius, and Helios. The specific composite nature of Serapis is new evidence for the existing argument about Hellenistic divine kingship frameworks.

The chapter argues Christianity used Greek frameworks (throughout). The specific evidence of Eleusinian/Bacchic mystery tropes (consuming the god through bread/wine, death-rebirth) from a rabbi's testimony is not in the inventory. Per QA, reframe through imperial cult. New evidence for existing argument.

Chapter 3 'Jesus is the Logos (lines 486-504)' explicitly covers Philo of Alexandria developing Logos theology, describing Logos as 'firstborn son of God' (De Confusione Linguarum 146), and its mirror in John's Gospel. Same argument, same evidence.

Chapter 3 covers apotheosis of Alexander (lines 574-580), Ptolemies (lines 581-587), and Augustus (lines 22-57, 610-619). Demetrius Poliorcetes receiving divine images in Athens alongside Demeter and Dionysus is not specifically mentioned, but this is covered in 'Evidence from inscriptions and coins (lines 610-619)' which documents Athens and Rhodes voting cult statues to Hellenistic rulers. The argument and category of evidence match.

The chapter does not discuss Carpocrates, Epiphanius (the young Gnostic), or Simon Magus teaching apotheosis/deification as salvific goals. This is a new argument (early Christian heterodox leaders explicitly taught Hellenistic apotheosis for initiates) with new evidence (Carpocrates' son, Epiphanius deified at 17, Simon Magus). Not in the chapter inventory.

Chapter 3 'Jesus is the Logos (lines 486-504)' covers Logos in John as Greek philosophical concept from Heraclitus, Stoics, and Philo. But the specific Logos-Zoe-Anthropos triad from Pythagorean/Middle Platonic philosophy (monad-dyad-triad framework) as the structural template for John 1 is not in the inventory. This is a more specific philosophical framework — new evidence for the existing Logos argument.

The chapter does not discuss the Hilaria Festival, Attis's death-resurrection celebration, or its calendrical overlap with Passover (March 15-27, Nissan 14-15). This is a new argument (Jesus's death-resurrection narrative was modeled on or competing with Attis's Hilaria) with new evidence (Claudius instituting Hilaria, March 25 = Annunciation day = Attis resurrection). Not in any chapter inventory.

Chapter 3 'Opening (lines 22-57)' covers Augustus as divi filius and 'cities hailed emperors as soter for grain, protection, peace.' The argument that 'Son of God' required doing something extraordinary for humanity and Augustus earned it by bringing peace is the same argument with the same evidence as the inventory.

Chapter 3 'Opening (lines 1-57)' includes 'Acts 17:6-7: politarchs of Thessalonica accuse missionaries of proclaiming another king, Jesus.' The argument that calling Jesus 'Son of God' or 'Lord' was political sedition (high treason against Rome) is present in the chapter through the Thessalonica evidence. Same argument.

The chapter covers Kingdom of God as political institution (lines 691-728) and kingdom language as political. But the specific argument that Jesus's healing work functioned as social-political reintegration (enabling community participation, work, status) rather than individual spiritual transformation is NOT made in the chapter. The kingdom-as-political evidence exists, but this particular argument about healing as political reintegration is new.

Chapter 3 'Jesus is the Son of Father (lines 476-485)' explicitly covers 'Plutarch: Plato's conception involved dynamis and pneuma' and 'Luke 1:35 uses same vocabulary (power and spirit) for Jesus's conception.' The finding about divine conception via Zeus/pneuma/spirit is the same argument with overlapping evidence. Perseus/Danae is a variant of the same covered category.

The chapter does not discuss the Last Supper's 'eat my flesh, drink my blood' language or argue it derives from Dionysian cult rather than Jewish Passover tradition. This is a new argument with new evidence. Per QA global decision, mystery cult framing should be reframed through imperial cult, but the data itself is new.

The chapter argues Christianity used Greek frameworks. The specific evidence about sacramental theology (ritual death and rebirth through participation in deity's death) coming from Osiris worship and entering Christianity through Paul is not in the inventory. Per QA, reframe through imperial cult. New evidence for existing argument.

Chapter 3 'Alexander the Great (lines 574-580)' covers 'Oracle at Siwa in Egypt (Plutarch, Lives 27)' and 'Ram's horns of Ammon on coinage.' The serpentine deity impregnating Olympias from the Alexander Romance is a variant telling of the same divine parentage tradition already covered.

The chapter does not discuss Isis as a salvation goddess whose promises (eternal life, divine protection, afterlife covenant) parallel early Christian theology. Isis cult is not in the chapter 3 inventory. This is a new argument (Isis cult as template for Christian salvation theology) with new evidence (Apuleius's Metamorphoses, Isis's promises of protection, afterlife, salvation).

The chapter does not discuss Isis as universal mother or syncretic identification with Demeter/Venus/Juno. The chapter covers Mary as dynastic mother (lines 640-690) but not Isis parallels. New argument with new evidence. Could potentially relate to chapter 3's Mary section but the Isis evidence and comparative argument are absent.

The chapter covers 'Messiah' as anointed royal title (lines 445-464) and anointing traditions. The specific Zerubbabel ceremony (two crowns of silver and gold, mutual anointing of governor and high priest) is not in the inventory. New evidence for the existing argument about anointing as royal-political practice.

The chapter argues 'Son of God' is Hellenistic royal terminology (throughout). The specific evidence about theosis/divinization as a widespread Hellenistic concept in mystery religions, and that Eastern Orthodox Christianity preserves theosis as a living doctrine, is not in the inventory. New evidence for the existing argument, though per QA the mystery cult frame should be reframed.

Chapter 3 'Alexander the Great (lines 574-580)' covers Alexander's deification and cult. The dynasty sections (574-609) and 'Evidence from inscriptions and coins (lines 610-619)' systematically document rulers deified through deeds. Same argument and evidence category.

The chapter does not argue that physical remains (bodies/tombs) possessed political power in the Hellenistic world or that Alexander's body was a strategic political asset. This is a new argument (resurrection as political-theological legitimacy connected to physical body as power) with new evidence (Alexander's body being fought over). Not in any chapter inventory.

Chapter 3 'Jesus is the Son of Father (lines 476-485)' covers 'only-begotten of the Father' as exclusive succession marker. Sanchuniathon's Phoenician cosmogony (king Kronos/El sacrificing his 'only begotten son' in royal apparel) is not in the inventory but supports the existing argument about 'only-begotten' as royal terminology. New evidence for existing argument.

Chapter 3 'Jesus is the Logos (lines 486-504)' explicitly covers Philo developing Logos theology, describing Logos as 'firstborn son of God' (De Confusione Linguarum 146), and its lineage to Christian theology. Same argument and evidence.

Chapter 3 covers Philo's Logos theology (lines 486-504) but does not mention Philo as a possible originator of 'consubstantiality' (homoousios) or 'union of natures.' This specific theological concept attributed to Philo is new evidence for the existing argument about Greek philosophical foundations of Christian theology.

The chapter does not argue that resurrection theology originated in Persian Zoroastrianism and was absorbed by Second Temple Judaism before entering Christianity. This is a new argument (Zoroastrian origin of resurrection) with new evidence (cross-cultural transmission path: Zoroastrianism -> Second Temple Judaism -> Christianity). Not in any chapter inventory.

Chapter 3 'Jesus is the Logos (lines 486-504)' covers Philo's Logos theology and its connection to Christian theology. The finding about Philo's 'triune nature of three' and Logos as 'divine heir' being 'strikingly similar' to what early Christians say about Jesus is the same argument and evidence.

Chapter 3 'Jesus is the Logos (lines 486-504)' covers Philo as Jewish aristocrat integrating Greek philosophy (Heraclitus, Stoics, Middle Platonists) with Judaism, and Christianity adopting this framework. Same argument, same evidence.

The chapter argues Gospel writers used Greek categories (Opening lines 47-50). MacDonald's specific analysis distinguishing royal anointing (christos) from burial anointing, and showing Mark 14:3-9 imitates Homer's Odyssey Book 19, is not in the inventory. New specific textual evidence for existing argument.

Same as finding 0 (mNv5JEoBcqI_2). Mark encoding Euroclea's name meaning ('fame/renown far and wide') into his narrative is specific new evidence for the existing argument about Greek literary framing. Not in inventory.

Chapter 3 'Jesus' birth was represented as a new star (lines 620-633)' covers 'Augustus: birth represented as new star; death/deification of Julius Caesar' and 'Opening (lines 22-57)' covers 'Augustus as divi filius on coins and civic festivals.' The finding about Caesar's apotheosis as a star and Augustus becoming 'son of god' through succession is the same evidence already in the inventory.
Chapter 3 'Jesus is the Logos (lines 486-504)' covers Philo's Logos theology before the NT. Same argument and evidence.

Chapter 3 'Anthropology: Jesus's doctrine of the soul (lines 127-144)' argues Jesus teaches the Greek immortal soul model, not Hebrew nefesh. The specific claim that heaven, hell, and purgatory come 'right out of Plato' and are 'not in the Hebrew Bible' is new evidence supporting the existing soul/afterlife argument. The chapter covers the soul distinction but not the heaven/hell/purgatory Platonic origin specifically.

Chapter 3 'Was God the Father the God of Moses or the God of Plato? (lines 97-126)' argues Paul and John describe the Father in Greek philosophical categories (Plato, Stoics). The specific claim that Greeks invented monotheism through Xenophanes and transmitted it via Genesis 1/Timaeus is new evidence for the existing argument about Greek philosophical origins of Christian theology.

This finding about Paul adapting Mithraic initiation rituals for the Christian Eucharist is more relevant to Chapter 5 (Pauline Epistles to All Nations), which covers Paul's theology and practices. Chapter 3 focuses on 'Son of God' as Hellenistic title and kingdom theology. Per QA global decision, mystery cult framing should be reframed through imperial cult.

Chapter 3 covers 'Son of God' as Hellenistic royal title (lines 465-474) and divine conception parallels (lines 476-485). The specific evidence of Apathus/Harpocrates ('the touched one' = 'anointed one without oil') born to Isis through miraculous conception is not in the inventory. New evidence for existing argument about divine sonship through miraculous conception.

Chapter 3 covers Augustus as divi filius (lines 22-57) and the Suetonius portents passage (lines 620-633). The specific vision of Octavius seeing his son with Jupiter's attributes (thunderbolt, scepter, crown, chariot with 12 white horses) is part of Suetonius Divus Augustus 94 which is referenced in the inventory. However, the specific details (thunderbolt, scepter, crown, 12 horses) are not enumerated. This is borderline covered but the specific imagery details are new evidence.

The chapter covers apotheosis across Hellenistic dynasties (lines 574-609). The specific evidence from Plutarch about figures dying, bodies disappearing, and being assumed divine — directly paralleled to Jesus's tomb narrative — is not enumerated in the inventory. New evidence for existing argument about resurrection as Hellenistic political-theological claim.

The chapter does not discuss Pythagorean gematria of Jesus's name (888) or Valentinian Christians who 'Platonized Pythagoras.' This is a new argument (Jesus's name was constructed through Greek numerical/philosophical frameworks) with new evidence (gematria value 888, Valentinian Pythagorean Christians). Not in any chapter inventory.

Chapter 3 covers Greek philosophical origins of Christian theology (lines 97-126, 486-504). The specific evidence from Plato's Timaeus about a 'god of dual nature connected by a third spiritual nature that mediates' as a pre-Christian trinitarian concept is not explicitly in the inventory. The Logos section cites Stoics and Heraclitus but not this specific Timaeus trinitarian passage. New evidence for existing argument.

Chapter 3 'Anthropology: Jesus's doctrine of the soul (lines 127-144)' argues Jesus teaches the Greek immortal soul, not Hebrew nefesh. The specific claim that heaven-above/hell-below cosmology originates from Greek/Roman religion and is 'found nowhere in the Old Testament or in Judaism' is new supporting evidence for the existing argument. The chapter covers the soul distinction but not the spatial cosmology origin.

Chapter 3 covers divine succession across Hellenistic dynasties (lines 574-609). The Derveni Papyrus (Europe's oldest surviving manuscript) using familial succession language ('Zeus took from his father the predicted Rule and strength') is not in the inventory. New primary source evidence for existing argument about divine kingship succession.

Chapter 3 'Ptolemaic Dynasty (lines 581-587)' covers Ptolemaic divine titles including 'Soter', 'Theoi Adelphoi', 'Euergetes', 'Epiphanes', and 'Jesus is the Son of God (lines 465-474)' covers 'Son of Ra' as pharaonic title. The Ptolemy V titulature (Heir of gods, Son of Ra, Living image of Amun) overlaps with the Rosetta Stone evidence already in the inventory.

Chapter 3 'Ptolemaic Dynasty (lines 581-587)' covers 'Ptolemy II Philadelphus + Arsinoe II: Theoi Adelphoi (Sibling Gods)' with cult establishment. The finding about Ptolemy I and Berenice I being inaugurated into divine realm by Ptolemy II who established a festival is the same evidence.

Chapter 3 argues Hellenistic royal authority was expressed through institutional markers (throughout). The specific evidence about Seleucid colonies being named after Macedonian cities (Amphipolis, Larissa, Europas) as continuous reminders of royal beneficence is not in the inventory. New evidence for existing argument about institutional expression of royal authority.

Chapter 3 'Seleucid Dynasty (lines 588-598)' explicitly covers 'Antiochus IV Epiphanes: God Manifest' and 'Jesus is the God manifested in flesh (lines 505-512)' covers epiphanic logic. Same argument and same evidence.

Chapter 3 'Seleucid Dynasty (lines 588-598)' covers Seleucid divine kingship with specific divine titles. The general argument about divine kingship being the 'ultimate expression of royal prerogative' is the same argument made throughout the dynasty sections (lines 574-609).

Chapter 3 'Ptolemaic Dynasty (lines 581-587)' covers Ptolemaic divine kingship with specific cult titles. The finding about Ptolemaic deification (being deified with temple and city, siblings exalted to divine status) is the same argument and evidence category.

Chapter 3 'Jesus is the Savior of the World (lines 546-552)' covers Soter as Hellenistic royal title (Ptolemy I Soter, Antiochus I Soter, Strato II Soter). 'Kyrios (Lord) (lines 553-562)' covers 'King of Kings' on Behistun Inscription. The finding about 'Soter' and 'king of the universe' (Akkadian sharru kishshati) is the same evidence.

Chapter 3 covers divine kingship across Ptolemaic (lines 581-587), Seleucid (lines 588-598), and other dynasties (lines 599-609). But the Nabataean kingdom is not mentioned. The deification of Nabataean king Abodas I with a Negev Desert inscription is new evidence — from Jesus's immediate geographic neighbors — for the existing argument about Hellenistic royal deification.

Chapter 3 'Ptolemaic Dynasty (lines 581-587)' explicitly covers 'Ptolemy II Philadelphus + Arsinoe II: Theoi Adelphoi (Sibling Gods).' The finding about 'Theo Adelphi' and 'Theo Philadelphi' cults is the same evidence.

Chapter 3 covers Seleucid dynasty (lines 588-598) including Antiochus I Soter. But the specific argument about temple building/restoration as a mechanism of royal legitimacy (the cylinder of Antiochus I Soter celebrating rebuilding the temple of Borsippa) is not in the inventory. New evidence for the existing argument about Hellenistic royal-theological practices.

Chapter 3 covers Seleucid titles including 'Antiochus III Megas' (lines 588-598). The specific evidence about competitive escalation of titles (Kullab inscription: 'basileos megistos' to outclass Antiochus's 'basileos megas') is not in the inventory. New evidence illustrating the competitive function of royal titulature.

Chapter 3 'Evidence from inscriptions and coins (lines 610-619)' covers 'Athens and Rhodes: voted cult statues and sacrifices to Hellenistic rulers.' The finding about Athens hailing Demetrius and Antigonus as 'kings and virtual demigods' with divine cult is the same evidence.

Chapter 3 'The Kingdom of God (lines 691-728)' covers basileia as Greek term for kingship/sovereignty. The specific evidence about the formal ceremonial adoption of the royal diadem as proclamation of Basileus (Antigonus and Demetrius) is not in the inventory. New evidence for existing argument about the institutional basis of Hellenistic kingship.
Chapter 3 'Jesus is the Savior of the World (lines 546-552)' covers 'Ptolemy I Soter' and the broader pattern of Soter as Hellenistic royal title. Same evidence.
Chapter 3 'Attalid Dynasty and Other Hellenistic dynasts (lines 599-609)' explicitly covers 'Attalus I Soter: divine cults (IvP I 15-19); Great Altar of Pergamon.' Same evidence.

Chapter 3 covers Attalus I Soter (lines 599-609) but does NOT mention the specific oracle describing the king as 'the loved Son of the god' raised by Zeus to defeat the Galatians, or the association with Delphi/Apollo. This oracle text explicitly using 'Son of the god' language for a Hellenistic ruler is new evidence for the existing argument about 'Son of God' as Hellenistic royal title.

Chapter 3 covers 'Son of Father' (Philopator) titles (lines 476-485) including Seleucus IV Philopator. But 'Eupater' (of noble father) for Antiochus V is not in the inventory. The specific example of Eupater being given to emphasize connection to sire for a very young king is new evidence for the existing argument about familial royal titles as legitimacy claims.

Chapter 3 covers Hellenistic royal authority and titles extensively, but the specific evidence about the diadem and signet ring as essential instruments of authority transfer and succession (Philip entrusted with royal signet ring and diadem) is not in the inventory. New evidence for existing argument about institutional expression of royal power.
Chapter 3 'Seleucid Dynasty (lines 588-598)' explicitly covers 'Antiochus III Megas: the Great (OGIS 245); divine honors (isotheoi timai) from Teos.' Same evidence.

Chapter 3 covers Ptolemaic divine kingship (lines 581-587) and 'Ptolemy I Soter: epithet Soter, initiated cult of Serapis.' But the specific propaganda story about Zeus as eagle rescuing infant Ptolemy to claim divine parentage and legitimacy to the Argead house is not in the inventory. New evidence for existing argument.
Chapter 3 'Jesus is the Savior of the World (lines 546-552)' covers Ptolemy I Soter. Same evidence.

Chapter 3 'Seleucid Dynasty (lines 588-598)' covers 'Antiochus IV Epiphanes: God Manifest' and 'Seleucid coins: radiate crowns, Zeus with star.' The finding about Antiochus IV with solar deity radiant crown on coins is the same evidence.

Chapter 3 'Seleucid Dynasty (lines 588-598)' covers 'Antiochus IV Epiphanes: God Manifest' and 'Seleucid coins: radiate crowns, Zeus with star.' Same evidence. The universalist cosmic imagery is a detail within the same covered topic.

Chapter 3 'Other Hellenistic dynasts (lines 599-609)' covers Bactrian 'Strato II Soter.' But the specific evidence that 'theos' was applied to living (not just deceased) Bactrian kings, with a tax document proving lifetime use for Antimachus I, is not in the inventory. New evidence for existing argument about Hellenistic divine epithets.
Chapter 3 'Alexander the Great (lines 574-580)' explicitly covers 'Oracle at Siwa in Egypt (Plutarch, Lives 27)' and 'Ram's horns of Ammon on coinage.' Same evidence.

Chapter 3 covers Alexander's divine claims (lines 574-580) but does not specifically discuss Alexander's strategic sacrifice to Egyptian gods as a political gesture leveraging anti-Persian sentiment. New evidence for existing argument about political-theological use of religious practice.

Chapter 3 covers Kingdom of God as political institution (lines 691-728) and cites 'Dan LXX 2:44.' But the specific evidence from Daniel's King of North/King of South being apocalyptic political categories (campaigns compared to natural disasters) is not in the inventory. New evidence for existing argument about kingdom language as political.

Chapter 3 'Alexander the Great (lines 574-580)' covers Alexander's divine parentage claims from Zeus-Ammon, including 'Arrian and Plutarch testify to divine self-understanding.' Same argument and evidence.

Chapter 3 'Seleucid Dynasty (lines 588-598)' covers 'Antiochus III Megas' and 'Kyrios (Lord) (lines 553-562)' covers 'King of Kings on Behistun Inscription.' The Babylonian/Persian/Assyrian royal titles are covered.

Chapter 3 covers 'Son of God' as royal legitimacy title across dynasties (lines 465-485, 574-609). But Andriscus (claiming to be illegitimate son of Perseus, accepted as king based on royal name and appearance) is not in the inventory. New evidence from Antigonid context for existing argument about filiation establishing monarchical legitimacy.

The chapter covers Hellenistic royal authority but does not specifically enumerate royal garments, diadem, and slaves as material symbols of kingship. New evidence for existing argument, similar to finding SIW18kYmnlM_2.

Chapter 3 'Evidence from inscriptions and coins (lines 610-619)' covers Hellenistic coinage but does not specifically mention Andriscus's coins with diadem portrait and Heracles lion-skin imagery. New specific numismatic evidence for existing argument.

Chapter 3 'Ptolemaic Dynasty (lines 581-587)' covers 'Ptolemy V Epiphanes (Rosetta Stone, 196 BC): Theos Epiphanes Eucharistos' and 'Jesus is the God manifested in flesh (lines 505-512)' covers the Epiphanes title. Same evidence.
Same as finding 83 (aoRA4W-9QBI_0). The tax document proving living use of 'theos' for Antimachus I is new evidence. Chapter covers Bactrian rulers generally but not this specific detail.

Chapter 3 'Other Hellenistic dynasts (lines 599-609)' covers Bactrian kingdoms but does not detail the strategic selection of different deities on coins (Zeus for Diotus, Heracles for Euthodmus, Poseidon for Eucrites). New evidence for existing argument about strategic deployment of divine imagery.

Chapter 3 'Ptolemaic Dynasty (lines 581-587)' covers Ptolemaic divine titles. The specific Hellenistic court poetry where Apollo prophesies Ptolemy II has 'the lofty blood of the saviors' with divine election language is not in the inventory. New evidence from literary propaganda for existing argument.

Chapter 3 covers Kingdom of God as political institution (lines 691-728). The specific Hellenistic court poetry describing Ptolemy's rule in cosmic terms (all rivers, lands, wealth, troops) paralleling Christian kingdom language is not in the inventory. New evidence for existing argument about universalist kingdom claims.

Chapter 3 'Seleucid Dynasty (lines 588-598)' covers 'divine honors (isotheoi timai) from Teos' for Antiochus III. The finding about kingship cult where mortal king wields divine power and receives isotheoitmai (honors equal to gods) is the same concept and evidence category.

Chapter 3 'Alexander the Great (lines 574-580)' covers Alexander's deification. The general principle that mortals could be exalted through great deeds (citing Heracles, Achilles, Dionysus) is covered through the dynasty sections and Justin Martyr's comparison (lines 47-50 — Heracles, Dionysus explicitly named).

Chapter 3 covers Hellenistic divine kingship but does not explicitly frame military victory as the Hellenistic equivalent of a 'mandate of heaven.' The concept that 'the gods clearly favored you if they deemed you worthy of victory' is not stated in the inventory as a distinct principle. New framing/evidence for existing argument.
Chapter 3 'Seleucid Dynasty (lines 588-598)' explicitly covers 'Seleucus I Nicator: claimed descent from Apollo (Appian, Roman History 56)' with omens and divine parentage. Same evidence.

Chapter 3 'Jesus is the Logos (lines 486-504)' covers John's Greek philosophical framework. But the specific 'divine images' concept (ubiquitous in Hellenistic world, manifesting deity's presence) as the lens for John's Christology is not named in the inventory. McClellan's 'image christology' framework is new evidence/framing for the existing argument about John using Greek categories.

Chapter 3 'The Kingdom of God (lines 691-728)' argues basileia is political sovereignty, and 'Jesus is the Savior of the World (lines 546-552)' covers Soter as political title. The argument that first-century Galileans understood 'salvation' as political-temporal deliverance from oppressors is the same argument about kingdom/salvation as political.

Chapter 3 argues 'Son of God' was a political royal title (lines 465-474). Psalm 45's explicit vocative address of the king as 'God' (Elohim) is not in the chapter 3 inventory (it appears in the cross-chapter ch5 inventory via Hebrews 1:8-9 connection but not in ch3). New evidence for the existing argument in chapter 3 about divine titles for human kings.

Chapter 3 argues 'Son of God' requires Greco-Christian rather than purely Jewish frameworks. The specific scholarly claim that 'no Jewish person prior to the 30s CE believed the Messiah would suffer and die and come back to life' is not in the inventory. This absence in Jewish tradition is new evidence supporting the need for an alternative (Greco-Christian) framework.

Chapter 3 'Jesus is the Logos (lines 486-504)' covers John's Gospel as rooted in Greek philosophy (Heraclitus, Stoics, Philo's Middle Platonism). The generic observation that 'John contains Platonic ideas' is covered.

The chapter does not discuss Daniel 7's Greek translation changing 'hos' to 'host' to conflate the Son of Man with the Ancient of Days. This is a new argument (Greek translation traditions shaped divine succession/identity categories applied to Jesus) with new evidence (the textual variant in LXX Daniel 7:14). Not in the chapter 3 inventory.

Chapter 3 argues Jesus's titles should be understood in Greco-Roman context. The specific 'Divine Images' concept (familiar from both Greco-Roman and Jewish contexts, manifesting divine power without ontological identity) is not named as a specific framework in the inventory. New conceptual evidence for existing argument.

Chapter 3 argues Jesus's titles operate as Hellenistic royal/divine designations. The specific Jewish tradition of Metatron and name-bearing angels exercising God's power and identifying with God through divine name possession is not in the inventory. New evidence for the existing argument about divine mediation frameworks.

Chapter 3 argues 'Son of God' is a royal title, not a claim to be God himself. The specific analysis of John 10:33 showing Jesus is accused of making himself 'divine' (not 'the God'), with his Psalm 82 defense, is not in the inventory. New textual evidence for the existing argument.

Chapter 3 covers 'Son of God' as Hellenistic royal title (lines 465-474). The specific exegesis of Hebrews 1:8-9 citing Psalm 45 where the human king is called Elohim as God's representative is not in the chapter 3 inventory. New evidence for existing argument.

Chapter 3 argues 'Son of God' is Hellenistic royal terminology. Peppard's scholarly framework of deity-humanity as a 'spectrum rather than a dichotomy' in the Greco-Roman world is not explicitly stated in the inventory though the argument is compatible. New scholarly framing/evidence for existing argument.

Chapter 3 covers Logos theology (lines 486-504) but does not use the specific framework of Jesus as 'authorized bearer of the Divine name' through Greco-Roman divine intermediary traditions. New conceptual evidence for existing argument.
Same as finding 107 (9g_yVZ6BszU_1). The 'Divine Images' concept as the key framework for understanding Jesus is not named in the chapter inventory. New evidence for existing argument.

Same category as finding 108 (9g_yVZ6BszU_2). Angel-of-the-Lord/name-bearing angel traditions in Greco-Roman Judaism as background for Christology. Not in chapter 3 inventory. New evidence for existing argument.

Chapter 3 'Jesus is the God manifested in flesh (lines 505-512)' and 'Jesus is the Logos (lines 486-504)' consistently argue for understanding titles in their original Greco-Roman context rather than through later philosophical frameworks. The methodological point about not retrojecting Trinitarian philosophy is the same approach the chapter already takes.

Chapter 3 covers Greek philosophical origins of Christian theology (lines 97-126). The specific claim about Trinity developing through homoousios ('same substance') from Greek philosophy and Christianity being 'wedded to Greek philosophy' to appeal to Roman intelligentsia is new evidence — the specific mechanism of Trinity development via homoousios is not in the inventory.

Chapter 3 covers God the Father through Greek philosophical categories (lines 97-126). The specific claim that immateriality/incorporeality of deity derives from Greek philosophical influence, with Christianity adopting it from Hellenistic Judaism while Rabbinic Judaism returned to the Hebrew Bible, is new evidence for the existing argument.

Chapter 3 covers Logos Christology (lines 486-504) but does not use the term 'image christology' or the specific argument that Jesus was 'conceptualized as a Divine image' using the 'logic of Divine Images' that 'just made sense to everybody.' New conceptual framework/evidence for existing argument.

Chapter 3 'Jesus is the Logos (lines 486-504)' covers the development of Logos theology from Greek philosophy through Philo to John. The finding about 'incorporating middle Platonic logos theology into the notion of Jesus as Messiah and Son of God' is the same argument.

Chapter 3 covers Logos as mediator (lines 486-504). The specific argument that the Angel of the Lord was a 'conceptual template' for framing Jesus's divine mediation is not in the inventory. New evidence for existing argument about Greek/Jewish intermediary traditions.
Same category as findings 107, 113, 118. The 'Divine Images' framework is not in chapter 3's inventory. New evidence for existing argument.
Same as finding 106 (9g_yVZ6BszU_0). Greek translation of Daniel 7 conflating Son of Man with Ancient of Days is a new argument with new evidence not in chapter 3 inventory.

Chapter 3 'Jesus is the Logos (lines 486-504)' covers Philo's Logos theology and John's adoption of it. The characterization of Logos as 'second God' (Philo) and 'another God' (Justin Martyr) as emanation from God is the same argument and evidence category.

Chapter 3 covers early Christian artistic depictions ('Why This Imagery Was Still Alive' lines 211-223, including Christ as Helios, Prima Porta Augustus pose). But the specific 4th-century sarcophagus evidence showing Jesus with a royal leafy wreath crown (not thorns), associated with military victory, is not in the inventory. New iconographic evidence for existing argument about Christians understanding Jesus through Hellenistic royal categories.

Chapter 3 covers kingdom language as political (lines 691-728, 757-870). The specific argument about the Beast/Revelation narrative encoding first-century political criticism of Roman emperors (not future prophecy) is not in the inventory but supports the existing political reading. New evidence for existing argument.

Chapter 3 argues 'Son of God' is Hellenistic (throughout). The specific argument that theosis/divinization was a Greco-Roman period innovation adopted by Christianity (not from Hebrew tradition) is not explicitly stated as a distinct claim in the inventory. New evidence for existing argument.

Chapter 3 covers 'Son of God' as title (lines 465-474). The specific argument about believers becoming 'sons of God' through Greco-Roman postmortem deification practices (ancestor worship + postmortem divine reward) is not in the inventory. New evidence for existing argument.

Chapter 3 covers Logos in John (lines 486-504). The specific Johannine theology of followers becoming 'sons of God' and achieving the same oneness with Jesus that Jesus has with God, understood as part of Greco-Roman deification tradition, is not in the inventory. New evidence for existing argument.

Chapter 3 'Opening (lines 51-53)' explicitly covers 'Psalm 2:7 functions as a royal investiture formula applied to Jesus at three events (annunciation, baptism, resurrection).' Same argument and evidence.

Chapter 3's entire thesis is that Jesus and early Christianity should be understood through Greco-Christian frameworks. The generic scholarly acknowledgment that 'there is a discussion to be had regarding Greco-Roman influence on resurrection and broader Jesus traditions' is the chapter's central argument, not new evidence.
Chapter 3 'Jesus is the Logos (lines 486-504)' covers John's Gospel as middle Platonic Logos theology. Same argument.

Chapter 3 'Kingdom of God as Crown of God (lines 697-728)' and 'Kingdom of God vs. Kingdom of Heaven (lines 735-756)' explicitly argue the Kingdom was understood as earthly and political, not heavenly/spiritual. Same argument.

Chapter 3 covers the Titles of Jesus section (lines 422-444) listing Son of Man as Aegean kingdom representative. But the specific argument about Daniel 7's Son of Man developing through Greco-Roman Jewish pseudepigraphic literature into a messianic divine mediator who possesses and exercises God's authority is not in the inventory's level of detail. New evidence for existing argument.

Chapter 3 covers Jesus's Greek soul doctrine (lines 127-144) including afterlife concepts. The specific evidence that Gehenna as postmortem divine punishment developed in the Greco-Roman period through figurative extension from a physical location is not in the inventory. New evidence for existing argument about Greek influence on Christian afterlife theology.

Chapter 3 'Was God the Father the God of Moses or the God of Plato? (lines 97-126)' explicitly argues God the Father was conceptualized through Greek philosophical categories. The finding about Christianity built on Greek/Platonic philosophy, especially regarding deity conceptualization, is the same argument.

Same category as findings about divine intermediaries (108, 114). The argument that Jesus's authority claims borrow from pre-existing Greco-Roman Jewish intermediary figures is not in the chapter 3 inventory at this level of specificity. New evidence for existing argument.

Chapter 3 covers 'Son of God' as title (lines 465-474). The specific argument that reading Proverbs 30:4 as referring to God's Son requires presupposing a Christian renegotiation of pre-Christian text is new evidence for the existing argument that 'Son of God' is not native to Hebrew tradition.

Chapter 3 'Jesus is the Logos (lines 486-504)' covers Logos theology in John. The specific grammatical argument about theos with/without article (ho theos = God of Israel; theos without article = divine, participating in divinity) is not in the inventory. New Greek grammatical evidence for existing argument.

Chapter 3 argues Hellenistic categories shaped Christian theology. The specific evidence that 'Pantocrator' was a Greek divine title borrowed into Judaism through the LXX (added without translating any Hebrew word) is not in the inventory. New evidence for existing argument about Greek titles entering Jewish-Christian tradition.

Chapter 3 presents Hellenistic titles (Son of God, Kyrios, Soter, etc.) throughout. But the specific methodological argument that titles are distinct from the later philosophical doctrines built upon them (e.g., Pantocrator title vs. doctrine of omnipotence) is a new argument about how to interpret titles. The evidence (Greek titles) already exists in the book, but this methodological distinction is not made.
Same category as findings 108, 114, 136. Metatron/divine name-bearing intermediaries in Greco-Roman Judaism. Not in chapter 3 inventory at this specificity. New evidence for existing argument.

Chapter 3 'Titles of Jesus (lines 422-444)' explicitly argues 'Jesus proclaimed with titles from all four [successor kingdoms]: Son of God, Lord, Messiah, Son of Man.' The finding about Jesus consolidating these traditions is the same argument.

Chapter 3 argues 'Son of God' requires Greco-Christian framework. The specific claim that mediatory figures who 'presence the deity and bear the deity's power' only became conceivable in Greco-Roman Judaism (not earlier) is new evidence for the existing argument. Not stated at this specificity in the inventory.

Chapter 3 covers Christian movement using Greek political idiom (lines 1-57). The specific parallel between Mark 1:1 ('beginning of the good news of Jesus Christ') and the Priene inscription (~9 BCE, 'beginning of the good news for the world' about Augustus) is not in the chapter 3 inventory. New evidence for existing argument about Gospel authors using imperial language.

Philippians 2 analysis belongs in Chapter 5 (Pauline Epistles to All Nations), which covers Paul's theology and letters. Chapter 3 focuses on 'Son of God' as Hellenistic title. The ch5 cross-chapter inventory likely addresses Pauline christology.
Same as finding 144 (XZ0moXB2bDk_0). Mark 1:1 / Priene inscription parallel. Not in chapter 3 inventory. New evidence for existing argument.

Chapter 3 covers Logos/intermediary theology (lines 486-504). The specific concept of 'binitarianism' in Greco-Roman Judaism (exalted humans or principal Angels as God's second-in-command) is not named in the inventory. New conceptual evidence for existing argument.
Same category as findings about divine name/intermediary traditions (108, 114, etc.). The 'communicable vehicle of divine agency' concept is not in the inventory. New evidence for existing argument.

Chapter 3 covers Son of Man in 'Titles of Jesus (lines 422-444)' as Aegean kingdom representative. The specific evidence that the NT 'son of man' concept draws directly from the Enochic tradition (within Hellenistic Judaism) is not in the inventory. New evidence for existing argument.
Chapter 3 'Jesus is the Logos (lines 486-504)' covers Logos theology from Philo to John. Same argument and evidence.

The chapter does not discuss the 'Jesus Barabbas' naming in Matthew 27:16 or the deliberate contrast between 'Jesus son of the father' and 'Jesus the Messiah.' This is a new argument (Gospel author consciously constructed titular contrasts using 'son' language) with new evidence (the Barabbas naming variant). Not in any chapter inventory.
Chapter 3 'Jesus is the Logos (lines 486-504)' covers Logos from Stoics and Middle Platonists. Same argument.

Chapter 3 covers Greek philosophical origins of Christian theology. The specific technical terms Logos Endiatetos vs. Logos Prooros from Justin Martyr, and Justin calling Jesus 'another God' multiple times, are not in the inventory. New evidence for existing argument.

Chapter 3 argues Greek philosophy shaped Christian theology (lines 97-126). The specific claim that 'the philosophical side of things took priority' over scripture in developing the Trinity is new evidence supporting the existing argument about primacy of Greek categories.
Chapter 3 'Jesus is the Logos (lines 486-504)' covers Philo's Logos as 'firstborn son of God' (De Confusione Linguarum 146). Philo's 'second God' terminology is the same evidence category.
Chapter 3 'Jesus is the Logos (lines 486-504)' covers Philo's Logos theology and its mirror in John's Gospel. Same argument.
Chapter 3 'Jesus is the Logos (lines 486-504)' covers Philo's Logos as mediator between divine and material, and John's parallel development. Same argument and evidence.
Chapter 3 'Opening (lines 22-57)' covers 'Augustus as divi filius on coins' and 'Jesus is the Son of God (lines 465-474)' covers 'Son of God' as Hellenistic royal title. Same argument.
Same as finding 138 (U8tEFvRwYbw_0). The Greek grammatical argument about article usage distinguishing 'God' from 'divine' in John 1:1c is not in the inventory. New evidence for existing argument.
Same category as findings about divine intermediary/name-bearing traditions (108, 114, etc.). Not in chapter 3 inventory. New evidence for existing argument.

Chapter 3 argues 'Son of God' is a political title (lines 465-474). The specific point that 'God' applied to rulers did not mean 'God the Father' but simply designated an entity as divine/deity is not explicitly argued in the inventory. New evidence for existing argument.

Chapter 3 argues God the Father is described in Greek rather than Jewish categories (lines 97-126). The specific scholarly claim that 'monotheism is not a productive analytical framework for looking at the Bible' is new evidence supporting the existing argument.

Chapter 3 argues the earliest Christian record is Greek (lines 87-96). The specific point about first-century Greek-Roman Jews interpreting prophecies through the Septuagint rather than Hebrew is not explicitly in the inventory. New evidence for existing argument about Greek framework primacy.
Same as findings 144, 146. The Priene inscription / Mark 1:1 parallel is not in chapter 3 inventory. New evidence for existing argument.
Same as finding 111 (Db0ESMV3pt0_0). Peppard's scholarly work on 'Son of God' in Roman context. Not in inventory. New evidence for existing argument.

Chapter 3 covers 'Son of God' as Hellenistic title (lines 465-474). Collins' scholarly work specifically examining how Hellenistic and Greco-Roman cultures influenced Jewish messianism is not in the inventory as a specific source. New scholarly evidence for existing argument.

Bird's work on the ontological relationship of Jesus to God by the time of John's composition is more relevant to Chapter 4 (Gospels as Historically Reliable), which covers Johannine theology and authorship. The cross-chapter ch4 inventory discusses John's theological development.

This finding about Paul's theology drawing on Greco-Roman Jewish concepts (logos, divine mediators, image christology) belongs in Chapter 5 (Pauline Epistles to All Nations), which covers Paul's theological framework and its Greco-Roman context.

Chapter 3 argues God the Father was described in Greek categories (lines 97-126). The specific argument that the Bible 'acknowledges numerous other gods from beginning to end' and monotheism is a 'post-biblical innovation' from Greek philosophy is new evidence for the existing argument about Greek philosophical foundations.

Chapter 3 covers the Lord's Prayer as fitting Egyptian solar-royal liturgy (lines 146-250) and the earliest record being Greek (lines 87-96). The specific claim that ALL Aramaic versions of the Lord's Prayer are translations from Greek is new supporting evidence for the existing argument about Greek primacy.

Chapter 3 covers Logos theology in John (lines 486-504). The specific exegesis of John 10 using Logos theology to articulate Jesus's divine sonship claim through Psalm 82 is not in the inventory. New evidence for existing argument.

Same category as findings 107, 113, 118, 121. The 'image christology' framework citing specific scholarly work (Alexi Kazazadon) on divine images in Mediterranean cult contexts is not in the inventory. New evidence for existing argument.
Philippians 2 analysis belongs in Chapter 5 (Pauline Epistles). Chapter 3 focuses on 'Son of God' as Hellenistic title; detailed Pauline text analysis is Chapter 5's domain.
Same as finding 171. John 10 / Psalm 82 Greco-Roman reinterpretation. Not in chapter 3 inventory. New evidence for existing argument.
Same category as findings 144, 146, 164. Priene inscription / Mark 1:1 parallel plus adoption christology. Not in chapter 3 inventory. New evidence for existing argument.
Same category as findings about image christology (107, 118, 172). Academic work on Divine Image logic governing Jesus conceptualization. Not in inventory. New evidence for existing argument.
Same as findings 171, 174. John 10 / Psalm 82 through Greco-Roman lens. Not in chapter 3 inventory. New evidence for existing argument.

Chapter 3 covers Hellenistic Soter title (lines 546-552) and Epiphanes title (lines 505-512). The specific exegesis of Titus 2:13 showing 'God and Savior' and 'great God and Savior' and 'epiphanies' were applied to Ptolemy, Augustus, and Julius Caesar is not in the inventory. New evidence connecting specific NT passage to Hellenistic titulature.

The chapter does not discuss Lucifer/phosphoros as a Greco-Roman Venus deity title applied to Jesus by Jerome in the Vulgate. This is a new argument (early Christian translators applied Greek religious terminology to Jesus) with new evidence (Jerome's choice of 'Lucifer' for phosphoros in NT morning star references). Not in any chapter inventory.

The analysis of Matthew's adaptation of Isaiah 7:14 through Greek translation (parthenos for 'young woman') is more relevant to Chapter 4 (Gospels as Historically Reliable) which covers Gospel composition and textual traditions. Chapter 3 focuses on 'Son of God' as Hellenistic title, not Gospel textual adaptation.

Same category as findings about divine intermediary traditions (108, 114, 141, etc.). Specific catalog of angel of the Lord, son of man, Yahoel, Metatron with divine names and worship — all consolidated in Jesus. Not in chapter 3 inventory. New evidence for existing argument.
Same as findings 111, 165. Peppard's scholarly work on 'Son of God' in Roman context applied to Mark. Not in inventory. New evidence for existing argument.

Chapter 3 'Jesus is the Lord (Kyrios) (lines 553-562)' covers Kyrios as imperial honorific and LXX substitution. But the specific argument about Mark deliberately exploiting the ambiguity of Kurios (generic lord vs. divine substitute) to suggest Jesus bears God's authority while remaining subordinate is not in the inventory at this level of detail. New evidence for existing argument.

Chapter 3 argues 'Son of God' is a royal title, not deity identification. The specific scholarly consensus (Joel Marcus, Eugene Boring, Adela Yarbro Collins, Daniel Johansson, Michael Pepper) that Mark and Matthew do not identify Jesus as God is not in the inventory. New scholarly evidence for existing argument.
Chapter 3 'Jewish Silence (lines 87-96)' explicitly covers 'Every extant Christian manuscript for the first two centuries is Greek.' Same argument.

Chapter 3 'Was God the Father the God of Moses or the God of Plato? (lines 97-126)' and Logos section (lines 486-504) cover Greek philosophical frameworks being employed to articulate Jesus's status. The finding about apologists making the gospel 'palatable to Greco Roman intelligentsia' is the same argument.
Same category as findings about 'image christology' / 'Divine Images' (107, 113, 118, 121, 172). Not in chapter 3 inventory. New evidence for existing argument.

Chapter 3 covers Logos in John (lines 486-504) and 'Son of God' as political title. The specific argument about John 10:30 ('I and the Father are one') being communicable to all followers (John 17 prayer) and therefore not a deity claim is not in the inventory. New evidence for existing argument.
Chapter 3 'Opening (lines 22-57)' covers 'Son of God' as pre-existing political term with Augustus as divi filius. Same argument.
Same category as findings about divine intermediary/image christology (108, 112, 114, 172). Not in chapter 3 inventory. New evidence for existing argument.
Same category as Divine Images findings. Not in chapter 3 inventory. New evidence for existing argument.

Same category as findings about divine intermediary traditions. The specific argument about 'Son of Man' authority claims rooted in Yahoel/Metatron traditions of authorized divine name bearers is not in the inventory. New evidence for existing argument.
Same as findings 144, 146, 164, 175. Priene inscription / Mark 1:1 parallel. Not in chapter 3 inventory. New evidence for existing argument.

This finding about Paul drawing from Greco-Roman Judaism, Greek metamorphosis narrative for incarnation, and Philo's Logos belongs in Chapter 5 (Pauline Epistles to All Nations), which covers Paul's theology.
Same as findings 144, 146, 164, 175, 193. Priene inscription / Mark 1:1 parallel. Not in chapter 3 inventory. New evidence for existing argument.
Chapter 3 'Jesus is the Logos (lines 486-504)' covers Philo's Logos as 'firstborn son of God' and the Logos tradition as framework for understanding Jesus. Same argument.

The chapter does not argue that Mark functions as an alternative founding myth competing with Virgil's Aeneid, motivated by anti-Roman political sentiment. This is a new argument (Mark as counter-Aeneid) with new evidence (Dennis McDonald's specific Aeneid parallels, anti-Roman motivation). Not in any chapter inventory.

Chapter 3 covers apotheosis across Hellenistic dynasties (lines 574-609) and Suetonius portents (lines 620-633). The specific structural parallels of 3-4 day intervals between death and apotheosis and the role of eyewitness testimony in Romulus/Caesar apotheosis narratives paralleling 1 Corinthians 15 is not in the inventory. New evidence for existing argument.
Chapter 3 'Opening (lines 22-57)' explicitly covers 'Augustus as divi filius on coins and civic festivals.' Same argument and evidence.
